comments:
Summary
This paper investigates a novel connection between sparse Bayesian learning (SBL) and gated recurrent neural networks. The authors show how SBL’s multi-loop majorization-minimization updates can be unfolded into LSTM-like and gated-feedback recurrent architectures, propose a Gated-Feedback LSTM (GFLSTM) surrogate, and train it (per dictionary) via supervised learning on synthetically generated data. Empirical results on synthetic correlated dictionaries, direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation, and photometric stereo indicate the learned networks can outperform SBL and other learning-based baselines in accuracy and run-time in challenging correlated regimes. The paper includes theoretical propositions and proofs linking the SBL iterations to the RNN gates and provides ablation studies.

Strengths
- Novel conceptual contribution: the detailed mapping from SBL iterations (with latent hyperparameters and inner/outer loops) to LSTM-style gates and the motivation to use gated-feedback architectures is both insightful and original. This extends the literature that translates iterative algorithms into learned networks.
- Sound technical development: the paper contains explicit derivations, definitions, and propositions (with proofs in the appendix) that justify the construction and monotonicity properties under stated conditions (e.g., choices of µ, α, β). The iterative reweighted perspective is well exploited to motivate gates and memory.
- Empirical work: evaluations include synthetic correlated dictionaries, an ablation study (gated feedback, cell type, model size), and two practical applications (DOA and photometric stereo). The online data generation heuristic and complexity/performance comparisons are useful.
- Good experimental indications that the learned models can outperform SBL on difficult correlated cases while being faster at test time.

Weaknesses and concerns
- Per-dictionary training assumption: the proposed approach trains a separate network for each dictionary Φ. The paper does not thoroughly explore generalization across dictionaries or how sensitive performance is to mismatch between training and test dictionaries. This limits practicality in applications where Φ varies or is high-dimensional.
- Computational cost and scalability: certain SBL-derived quantities (w(t), u(t)) require inverses involving Σy = ΦΓΦ> + λI. Although the authors propose computing approximations via subnetworks, the paper provides limited detail and empirical analysis on the training and inference cost for large-scale problems (large m, n) and whether the approach scales favorably compared to alternatives when Φ is large.
- Dependence on synthetic training distribution: training uses online synthetic samples with randomly drawn sparse supports and amplitudes. While effective here, it remains unclear how robustly the learned strategy transfers to real-world data distributions that differ from the training generator; more analysis or domain adaptation discussion would strengthen the paper.
- Presentation density: while generally clear and organized, parts of the paper are notation-heavy and require careful reading (e.g., the sequence of bounds and variational parameters in Appendix E). Some practical implementation details (exact architectures, training times for all comparisons, and how complex-valued operations are realized) are deferred to appendices; brief summarization in the main text would help comprehension.

Suggestions for improvement
- Include experiments that test generalization across different dictionaries: for example, train on a family of dictionaries with varying correlation structures and evaluate on held-out, unseen dictionaries to assess transferability.
- Provide a more detailed runtime and memory comparison (training and inference) for larger-scale settings; quantify the cost of computing the SBL-inspired quantities when implemented exactly versus via learned subnetworks.
- Add an experiment exploring robustness to distribution mismatch between training sample generation (amplitude/noise/spread) and real test scenarios (especially for photometric stereo/DOA).
- Clarify practical guidelines: how many unrolled steps, network size choices, and when one must retrain for a new Φ.

Overall recommendation and rationale
This paper makes a meaningful and original contribution by revealing and exploiting a deep connection between SBL and gated recurrent nets, and demonstrates practical benefits in challenging correlated sparse-estimation regimes. The theoretical grounding and empirical evaluations are solid, though practical limitations (per-dictionary training, scaling) deserve more discussion. I recommend acceptance (high score) because the ideas are likely to inspire further research on learned multi-loop optimizers and structured RNNs for inverse problems.
